---
title: "13. Residual Depth Evaluation"
---

## Goal

This note evaluates the current `checkpoints/residual_depth.pt` checkpoint
against several opponents. The purpose is not to declare a final strength
rating. It is to learn what changed after moving from the plain MLP to a
depth-only residual MLP.

The checkpoint metadata is:

| field | value |
|---|---:|
| completed self-play games | 3000 |
| model type | residual |
| hidden size | 128 |
| residual blocks | 3 |
| training simulations | 250 |
| opening plies | 0..8 |
| replay capacity | 50000 |

The architecture is the depth-only experiment:

$$
\text{residual MLP, hidden size } 128, \text{ blocks } 3.
$$

That means this run tests depth while keeping the hidden width equal to the
original MLP.

## Evaluation Setup

All evaluations used the same random-opening distribution:

$$
k \sim \operatorname{Uniform}\{0,1,\ldots,8\}.
$$

For each paired opening, the evaluator reused the same opening position with
the players swapped. This reduces the chance that one agent gets easier
openings by accident.

The neural agent used:

- batched Monte Carlo Tree Search,
- evaluation batch size 8,
- the optional C++ game engine,
- deterministic action selection from search visit counts.

The common command shape was:

```bash
python scripts/evaluate.py \
  --checkpoint-a checkpoints/residual_depth.pt \
  --agent-b minimax \
  --games 200 \
  --simulations 250 \
  --batched-mcts \
  --eval-batch-size 8 \
  --fast-game \
  --opening-plies-min 0 \
  --opening-plies-max 8
```

The script reports a win rate:

$$
\widehat{w} = \frac{\text{wins}}{\text{games}}.
$$

For comparison, the table below also includes score rate, which gives half
credit for draws:

$$
\widehat{s} =
\frac{\text{wins} + \frac{1}{2}\text{draws}}{\text{games}}.
$$

## Results

| opponent | games | simulations | result | win rate | score rate |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| random | 200 | 100 | 199-1-0 | 0.995 | 0.995 |
| greedy | 200 | 100 | 150-30-20 | 0.750 | 0.800 |
| MCTS with uniform evaluator | 100 | 100 | 91-5-4 | 0.910 | 0.930 |
| minimax | 200 | 100 | 42-149-9 | 0.210 | 0.233 |
| minimax | 200 | 250 | 62-131-7 | 0.310 | 0.328 |
| minimax | 200 | 400 | 74-107-19 | 0.370 | 0.417 |
| noisy minimax, $\epsilon=0.1$ | 200 | 100 | 64-127-9 | 0.320 | 0.343 |

The result notation is:

$$
\text{agent wins} - \text{opponent wins} - \text{draws}.
$$

## Commands

Random:

```bash
python scripts/evaluate.py \
  --checkpoint-a checkpoints/residual_depth.pt \
  --agent-b random \
  --games 200 \
  --simulations 100 \
  --batched-mcts \
  --eval-batch-size 8 \
  --fast-game \
  --opening-plies-min 0 \
  --opening-plies-max 8
```

Greedy:

```bash
python scripts/evaluate.py \
  --checkpoint-a checkpoints/residual_depth.pt \
  --agent-b greedy \
  --games 200 \
  --simulations 100 \
  --batched-mcts \
  --eval-batch-size 8 \
  --fast-game \
  --opening-plies-min 0 \
  --opening-plies-max 8
```

Uniform MCTS:

```bash
python scripts/evaluate.py \
  --checkpoint-a checkpoints/residual_depth.pt \
  --agent-b mcts \
  --games 100 \
  --simulations 100 \
  --batched-mcts \
  --eval-batch-size 8 \
  --fast-game \
  --opening-plies-min 0 \
  --opening-plies-max 8
```

Deterministic minimax:

```bash
python scripts/evaluate.py \
  --checkpoint-a checkpoints/residual_depth.pt \
  --agent-b minimax \
  --games 200 \
  --simulations 100 \
  --batched-mcts \
  --eval-batch-size 8 \
  --fast-game \
  --opening-plies-min 0 \
  --opening-plies-max 8

python scripts/evaluate.py \
  --checkpoint-a checkpoints/residual_depth.pt \
  --agent-b minimax \
  --games 200 \
  --simulations 250 \
  --batched-mcts \
  --eval-batch-size 8 \
  --fast-game \
  --opening-plies-min 0 \
  --opening-plies-max 8

python scripts/evaluate.py \
  --checkpoint-a checkpoints/residual_depth.pt \
  --agent-b minimax \
  --games 200 \
  --simulations 400 \
  --batched-mcts \
  --eval-batch-size 8 \
  --fast-game \
  --opening-plies-min 0 \
  --opening-plies-max 8
```

Noisy minimax:

```bash
python scripts/evaluate.py \
  --checkpoint-a checkpoints/residual_depth.pt \
  --agent-b noisy-minimax \
  --games 200 \
  --simulations 100 \
  --batched-mcts \
  --eval-batch-size 8 \
  --fast-game \
  --opening-plies-min 0 \
  --opening-plies-max 8 \
  --noise-prob 0.1
```

## Interpretation

The residual-depth checkpoint easily beats random play, greedy play, and
uniform-evaluator MCTS on this randomized-opening distribution. That is a good
basic sanity check: the network and search are learning something stronger than
the simple baselines.

The hard result is deterministic minimax. The residual checkpoint improves as
the search budget increases:

$$
0.210 \rightarrow 0.310 \rightarrow 0.370
$$

for 100, 250, and 400 simulations. This means extra search still helps, so the
network is not completely misleading MCTS. But even at 400 simulations the agent
is below parity against minimax.

The noisy-minimax result is also informative. With a $10\%$ random-move error
rate, the agent scores better than it does against deterministic minimax at the
same 100-simulation budget:

$$
0.210 \rightarrow 0.320.
$$

That suggests the agent can exploit some mistakes, but deterministic minimax is
still exposing tactical weaknesses.

## Comparison With The Plain MLP

The current plain MLP checkpoint was previously evaluated on the same
`0..8` opening distribution after 5800 games:

| checkpoint | simulations | result vs minimax | win rate |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| plain MLP | 100 | 53-142-5 | 0.265 |
| plain MLP | 250 | 83-113-4 | 0.415 |
| plain MLP | 400 | 80-113-7 | 0.400 |
| residual depth | 100 | 42-149-9 | 0.210 |
| residual depth | 250 | 62-131-7 | 0.310 |
| residual depth | 400 | 74-107-19 | 0.370 |

This is not a perfectly controlled comparison because the plain MLP had 5800
self-play games, while `residual_depth.pt` has 3000. Still, the residual-depth
checkpoint has not yet shown a clear advantage over the plain MLP.

The next fair experiment is to continue the residual-depth checkpoint to a
similar training budget before judging the architecture:

```bash
python scripts/train.py \
  --resume checkpoints/residual_depth.pt \
  --games 5800 \
  --simulations 250 \
  --epochs 1 \
  --batched-mcts \
  --eval-batch-size 8 \
  --fast-game \
  --opening-plies-min 0 \
  --opening-plies-max 8 \
  --replay-capacity 50000
```

Then rerun the same minimax evaluation table. If the residual model still lags
after equal training, depth alone is probably not the right upgrade. The next
controlled experiment would be width-only:

$$
\text{plain MLP, hidden size } 256.
$$
